questions.scenario: Ashgrove Pointe Securities Inc., an Investment Dealer, is onboarding four prospective clients in the same week, and designated Supervisor Veronique Caza is reviewing each file before approval. File 1: retail client Omar Haddad wants an advisory account and a margin account; the draft margin account agreement he is about to sign describes his repayment and interest obligations but says nothing about the dealer's right to sell assets in his account or make purchases to cover short sales. File 2: retail client June Park wants a discretionary account; the draft discretionary account agreement contains an automatic-renewal clause extending the 12-month term for successive 12-month periods unless either party objects. File 3: retail client Stellan Brandt wants a derivatives account to trade equity options in an advisory relationship; the file contains a completed derivatives account application but no derivatives trading agreement and no record yet of designated Supervisor approval. File 4: Cordova Stevedoring Ltd., a non-individual with $14 million of securities under management, asks to be treated as an institutional client.
questions.stem: File 1: Omar's draft margin account agreement covers his repayment and interest obligations but is silent on the dealer's right to sell assets in his account or make purchases to cover short sales. Measured against subsection 3247(2), Veronique should conclude the agreement is:

options.A: Acceptable — a margin account agreement need only describe the client's obligations, since the dealer's rights arise automatically by law
options.B: Acceptable, provided the missing rights are explained verbally before the first margin call
options.C: Deficient: the margin account agreement must, at a minimum, contain a written description of the Dealer Member's right to sell assets in the client's account and make purchases to cover short sales, including any notice the client requires and the client's obligations to remedy a deficiency
options.D: Deficient only if Omar intends to short sell

questions.rationale: Clause 3247(2)(v) requires the margin account agreement to contain, at a minimum, a written description of the Dealer Member's right to sell assets in the client's account and make purchases to cover short sales, and where the client requires prior notice, the nature of the notice and the client's obligations to remedy any deficiency — 3247(2) prescribes a two-sided minimum content list covering both client obligations and dealer rights. Option A is the closest distractor — the rule expressly demands a written description of the dealer-rights items, so reliance on background law fails the minimum-content standard; B substitutes a verbal explanation for required written content; D wrongly conditions a universal content item on the client's shorting intentions.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member's right to sell assets in the client's account and make purchases to cover short sales. If the client requires prior notice, the Dealer Member must set out the nature of the notice and the client's obligations to remedy any deficiency," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5136

questions.scenario: Ashgrove Pointe Securities Inc., an Investment Dealer, is onboarding four prospective clients in the same week, and designated Supervisor Veronique Caza is reviewing each file before approval. File 1: retail client Omar Haddad wants an advisory account and a margin account; the draft margin account agreement he is about to sign describes his repayment and interest obligations but says nothing about the dealer's right to sell assets in his account or make purchases to cover short sales. File 2: retail client June Park wants a discretionary account; the draft discretionary account agreement contains an automatic-renewal clause extending the 12-month term for successive 12-month periods unless either party objects. File 3: retail client Stellan Brandt wants a derivatives account to trade equity options in an advisory relationship; the file contains a completed derivatives account application but no derivatives trading agreement and no record yet of designated Supervisor approval. File 4: Cordova Stevedoring Ltd., a non-individual with $14 million of securities under management, asks to be treated as an institutional client.
questions.stem: File 2: June's discretionary account agreement with its automatic-renewal clause. Under subsection 3274(1), what must Veronique require?

options.A: Nothing — renewal clauses are standard and acceptable in discretionary agreements
options.B: Extension of the term to 24 months so renewals are less frequent
options.C: Removal of the clause: a discretionary account agreement must have a maximum term of no longer than 12 months and must not be renewable
options.D: Conversion of the renewal clause into a 60-day evergreen notice provision

questions.rationale: Clauses 3274(1)(iii) and (iv) require a discretionary account agreement to have a maximum term of no longer than 12 months and to not be renewable — an evergreen automatic-renewal clause violates the non-renewability requirement directly; a fresh agreement is the only path to continued discretion. Option B doubles the maximum term the rule caps at 12 months; D repackages renewal as an evergreen notice, which is still renewal; A accepts a clause the rule forbids.
questions.source_quote: "(iii) have a maximum term of no longer than 12 months, (iv) not be renewable, and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5373

questions.scenario: Ashgrove Pointe Securities Inc., an Investment Dealer, is onboarding four prospective clients in the same week, and designated Supervisor Veronique Caza is reviewing each file before approval. File 1: retail client Omar Haddad wants an advisory account and a margin account; the draft margin account agreement he is about to sign describes his repayment and interest obligations but says nothing about the dealer's right to sell assets in his account or make purchases to cover short sales. File 2: retail client June Park wants a discretionary account; the draft discretionary account agreement contains an automatic-renewal clause extending the 12-month term for successive 12-month periods unless either party objects. File 3: retail client Stellan Brandt wants a derivatives account to trade equity options in an advisory relationship; the file contains a completed derivatives account application but no derivatives trading agreement and no record yet of designated Supervisor approval. File 4: Cordova Stevedoring Ltd., a non-individual with $14 million of securities under management, asks to be treated as an institutional client.
questions.stem: File 3: Stellan's derivatives account. The desk wants to execute his first options trade this afternoon on the strength of the completed derivatives account application alone. Under subsection 3251(1), what remains outstanding before any initial derivatives transaction?

options.A: Nothing — the application alone authorizes the first trade, with remaining documents due within 25 business days
options.B: A completed and signed derivatives trading agreement from the client, provision of the most recent derivatives disclosure statement, and the relevant designated Supervisors' approval recorded in writing
options.C: Only an oral briefing on option assignment mechanics
options.D: A letter of undertaking, which retail clients may substitute for the trading agreement

questions.rationale: Subsection 3251(1) requires, before executing an initial derivatives transaction in any account type, that the dealer obtain a completed derivatives account application, obtain a completed and signed derivatives trading agreement, provide the most recent derivatives disclosure statement or similar document, and record the relevant designated Supervisors' approval in writing — only the first item is in Stellan's file. Option D is the closest distractor — under 3253(1) the letter of undertaking substitutes for the trading agreement only where the client is classified as an institutional client, which Stellan is not; A imports the 3213(2)(iii) document-chasing window into a precondition rule; C replaces prescribed documents with a briefing.
questions.source_quote: "(ii) obtain a completed and signed derivatives trading agreement from the client, (iii) provide the client with the most recent derivatives disclosure statement or similar disclosure document, and (iv) record the relevant designated Supervisors' approval in writing." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5175

questions.scenario: Ashgrove Pointe Securities Inc., an Investment Dealer, is onboarding four prospective clients in the same week, and designated Supervisor Veronique Caza is reviewing each file before approval. File 1: retail client Omar Haddad wants an advisory account and a margin account; the draft margin account agreement he is about to sign describes his repayment and interest obligations but says nothing about the dealer's right to sell assets in his account or make purchases to cover short sales. File 2: retail client June Park wants a discretionary account; the draft discretionary account agreement contains an automatic-renewal clause extending the 12-month term for successive 12-month periods unless either party objects. File 3: retail client Stellan Brandt wants a derivatives account to trade equity options in an advisory relationship; the file contains a completed derivatives account application but no derivatives trading agreement and no record yet of designated Supervisor approval. File 4: Cordova Stevedoring Ltd., a non-individual with $14 million of securities under management, asks to be treated as an institutional client.
questions.stem: File 4: Cordova Stevedoring's request for institutional classification. Veronique checks the Rule 1201 definition and section 3212. Which analysis is correct?

options.A: Cordova qualifies as a non-individual with total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million, and the dealer must verify that the client qualifies as an institutional client
options.B: Cordova cannot qualify because only registrants, banks and regulated entities can be institutional clients
options.C: Cordova qualifies automatically; no verification by the dealer is needed once the client asserts the threshold
options.D: Cordova must first qualify as a hedger before any institutional classification is possible

questions.rationale: The Rule 1201 institutional client definition includes a non-individual with total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million — Cordova's $14 million clears it — and subsection 3212(2) requires that, for each institutional client, the Dealer Member verify that the client qualifies as an institutional client. Option C is the closest distractor — qualification under the definition does not remove the dealer's express verification duty; B truncates the definition to its regulated-entity limbs; D confuses the separate hedger route in limb (vii), which is one path for hedgers, not a gateway for all non-individuals.
questions.source_quote: "a non-individual with total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 481
